revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|Japanese book |author=Satoshi Ito|authorlink={{ill|Satoshi Ito|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=What is Shinto? |year=2012 |publisher=[[Chuo Koron Shinsha]] |series=[[Chuko Shinsho]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Ryobu Torii|ja|両部鳥居}} * {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Esoteric Buddhism}} {{Buddhism}}

-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Religion-stub}}

These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|12=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|12=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|12=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|12=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|12=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|12=draft}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|12=draft}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|12=draft}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|12=draft}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|12=draft}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|12=draft}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|12=draft}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|12=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|12=draft}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|12=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|12=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|12=draft}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|12=draft}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|12=draft}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|12=draft}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|12=draft}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|12=draft}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|12=draft}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|12=draft}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|12=draft}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|12=draft}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|12=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|12=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|12=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|12=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|12=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|12=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|12=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|12=draft}}, are likened to the two mandalas.

With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|12=draft}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|12=draft}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|12=draft}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|12=draft}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|12=draft}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|12=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|12=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|12=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|12=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|12=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|12=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|12=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|Japanese book |author=Satoshi Ito|authorlink={{ill|Satoshi Ito|ja|伊藤聡|12=draft}} |title=What is Shinto? |year=2012 |publisher=[[Chuo Koron Shinsha]] |series=[[Chuko Shinsho]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Ryobu Torii|ja|両部鳥居|12=draft}} * {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Esoteric Buddhism}} {{Buddhism}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|天照大神|" to "|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|"
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|12=draft}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|12=draft}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|12=draft}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|12=draft}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|12=draft}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|12=draft}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|12=draft}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|12=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|12=draft}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|12=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|12=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|12=draft}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|12=draft}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|12=draft}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|12=draft}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|12=draft}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|12=draft}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|12=draft}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|12=draft}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|12=draft}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|12=draft}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|12=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|12=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|12=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|12=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|12=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|12=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|12=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|12=draft|12=draft}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|12=draft|12=draft}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|12=draft|12=draft}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|12=draft|12=draft}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|12=draft|12=draft}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|12=draft|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|12=draft|12=draft}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|12=draft|12=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|12=draft|12=draft}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|12=draft|12=draft}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|12=draft|12=draft}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|12=draft|12=draft}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|12=draft|12=draft}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft|12=draft}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft|12=draft}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|12=draft|12=draft}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|12=draft|12=draft}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft|12=draft}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|12=draft|12=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|12=draft|12=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|12=draft|12=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|12=draft|12=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|12=draft|12=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|12=draft|12=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|12=draft|12=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|Japanese book |author=Satoshi Ito|authorlink={{ill|Satoshi Ito|ja|伊藤聡|12=draft|12=draft}} |title=What is Shinto? |year=2012 |publisher=[[Chuo Koron Shinsha]] |series=[[Chuko Shinsho]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Ryobu Torii|ja|両部鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Esoteric Buddhism}} {{Buddhism}}

Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

revision.comment: 43 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:両部神道 up to rev 104084080
revision.text:
{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:16 (UTC)|参照方法=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:16 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|金剛界曼荼羅]] [[ファイル:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|胎蔵曼荼羅]] '''両部神道'''（りょうぶしんとう）とは、[[仏教]]の[[真言宗]]（密教）の立場からなされた[[神道]]解釈に基づく[[神仏習合|神仏習合思想]]である。'''両部習合神道'''（りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう）ともいう。
==概要== [[密教]]では、宇宙は[[大日如来]]の顕現であるとされる。それは大日如来を中心にした[[両界曼荼羅#金剛界曼荼羅|金剛界曼陀羅]]と[[両界曼荼羅#胎蔵曼荼羅|胎蔵曼陀羅]]の儀規として表現されている。この金剛界と胎蔵界の両部の[[曼陀羅]]に描かれた[[仏]][[菩薩]]を本地とし、[[神 (神道)|日本の神々]]をその垂迹として解釈した。
===思想=== 両部神道では、伊勢内宮の祭神、[[天照大神]]は胎蔵界の大日如来であり、[[梵天|光明大梵天王]]であり、[[十二天|日天子]]であるとし、一方、伊勢外宮の[[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]は、金剛界の大日如来であり、[[梵天|尸棄大梵天王]]であり、[[十二天|月天子]]であるとする。そして[[伊勢神宮]]の内宮と外宮は胎蔵界と金剛界の両部で、この両部が一体となって大日如来の顕現たる伊勢神宮を形成しているとした（二宮一光説）。両部神道とは、これによって神と仏の究極的一致を説明しようとしたところに注目した命名である<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |publisher=中央公論新社 |series=中公新書 |year=2012}}</ref>。
また、[[日本書紀]]の三神に、仏教の[[如来]]の[[三身]]をあてはめ、[[国之常立神|国常立尊]]が[[法身]]、[[国狭槌尊]]が[[報身]]、[[トヨクモノ|豊斟渟尊]]が[[応身]]であるとし、この三神が合一して、密教の[[本尊]]である大日如来となるともした。
また[[古事記]]の[[神世七代|天神七代]]は[[七仏通誡偈|過去七仏]]に等しく、また[[北斗七星]]の各星を表しているとされた。また[[イザナギ]]・[[イザナミ]]、[[諏訪神社]]の上社・下社、なども両部曼陀羅になぞらえられた。
==歴史== 両部神道の萌芽は仏教伝来にまでたどることができる。
[[仏教公伝|仏教伝来]]により日本古来の信仰であった神道も多大な影響を受けた。日本の神々も仏法による[[解脱]]を望んでいるとして神前読経が行われるようになり、神社の境内に[[神宮寺]]が建てられ、[[仏像]]の影響を受けて[[神像]]も製作されるようになった。
やがて[[8世紀]]末頃から、日本の神々は仏と同体と考えられ、本地である仏が日本の人々を救済するために仮に神に姿を変えて現れたとする[[本地垂迹]]説が発生し、のちの[[神仏習合]]思想の基礎となった。
[[平安時代]]後期には、神道を理論的に説明する教説として僧侶による仏家の神道理論が成立した。当時の仏教界の主流であった密教二宗のうち、[[天台宗]]の教えを取り入れたのが[[山王神道]]、真言宗の教えを取り入れたのが'''両部神道'''である。
両派とも[[大祓詞]]の解説や、[[日本神話|記紀神話]]などに登場する神や神社の祭神の説明が、当時の仏教界の主流だった密教の教義を用いてなされている。
いずれも、[[最澄]]・[[空海]]などに選者を仮託する神道書によっており、各神社の秘伝として伝授され、また一部は、[[修験道]]などを介して民間にも知られていった。これらは[[鎌倉時代]]に理論化され、後世多くの神道説を生み出していった。
これらの神道書のうち、後世に最も大きな影響を与えたのが、醍醐天皇が神泉苑に出現した龍女から受けた秘伝と称する『[[麗気記]]』である。この書は、伊勢神宮に関する真言密教に基づいた深秘説を集成しており、南北朝期以降、『日本書紀』と並ぶ中世神道の最も重要な聖典と見なされるようになった<ref name="itou"/>。
===影響=== 両部神道はのちの神道説の展開に大きな影響をあたえ、中世には習合神道説の主流となって、[[御流神道]]、[[三輪神道]]などの多くの分流が生じた。
しかし、鎌倉時代末期から[[南北朝時代_(日本)|南北朝時代]]になると、僧侶による神道説に対する反動から、逆に、神こそが本地であり仏は仮の姿であるとする[[神本仏迹説]]をとなえる[[伊勢神道]]や[[吉田神道]]が現れ、江戸時代には神道の主流派の教義となっていく。
[[幕末]]から[[明治維新]]にかけ、[[明治元年]]（1868年）に出された[[廃仏毀釈]]および[[神仏分離]]により両部神道は壊滅的な打撃を受け、神道教義の主流派の地位を失った。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書 |author=伊藤聡|authorlink=伊藤聡 |title=神道とは何か |year=2012 |publisher=[[中央公論新社]] |series=[[中公新書]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8 }}
== 関連項目 == * [[両部鳥居]] * [[神仏習合]] {{中世神道}} {{密教2}} {{Buddhism2}} {{Normdaten}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Reli-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:りようふしんとう}} [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:神道の流派]] [[Category:真言系仏教]] [[Category:日本の密教]] [[Category:密教]] [[Category:平安時代の仏教]]

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

[[ja:両部神道]] [[en:Ryobu Shinto]] [[sn:Ryobu Shinto]] [[zh:兩部神道]] [[ru:Рёбу синто]] [[fr:Ryōbu shintō]] [[sn:yobu Shinto]]

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|Japanese book |author=Satoshi Ito|authorlink={{ill|Satoshi Ito|ja|伊藤聡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |title=What is Shinto? |year=2012 |publisher=[[Chuo Koron Shinsha]] |series=[[Chuko Shinsho]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Ryobu Torii|ja|両部鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Esoteric Buddhism}} {{Buddhism}}

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).

Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.

It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu-ji|qq=}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8th century|qq=}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Shinto|qq=}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oharae no Kotoba|qq=}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saicho|qq=}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kukai|qq=}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendo|qq=}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen-en|qq=}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period|qq=}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|qq=}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Shinto|qq=}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu|qq=}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abolition of Buddhism|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Ise Shinto]] → [[Ise Grand Shrine]]
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist|qq=}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon sect|qq=}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhism|qq=}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|qq=}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese deities|qq=}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Light King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trikaya|qq=}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dharmakaya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sambhogakaya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nirmanakaya|qq=}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu-ji|qq=}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8th century|qq=}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Shinto|qq=}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oharae no Kotoba|qq=}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saicho|qq=}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kukai|qq=}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendo|qq=}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen-en|qq=}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period|qq=}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|qq=}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Shinto|qq=}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist|qq=}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon sect|qq=}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
