This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese deities|qq=}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Light King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trikaya|qq=}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dharmakaya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sambhogakaya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nirmanakaya|qq=}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu-ji|qq=}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8th century|qq=}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Shinto|qq=}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oharae no Kotoba|qq=}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saicho|qq=}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kukai|qq=}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendo|qq=}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen-en|qq=}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period|qq=}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|qq=}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Shinto|qq=}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu|qq=}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abolition of Buddhism|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist|qq=}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon sect|qq=}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhism|qq=}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|qq=}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese deities|qq=}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Light King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trikaya|qq=}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dharmakaya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sambhogakaya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nirmanakaya|qq=}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.

These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saichō|lt=Saichō|lt=Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saicho|qq=}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kukai|qq=}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendo|qq=}}. Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen-en|qq=}}. During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu|qq=}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abolition of Buddhism|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] → [[Shinbutsu bunri]]
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist|qq=}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon sect|qq=}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhism|qq=}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|qq=}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese deities|qq=}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Light King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trikaya|qq=}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dharmakaya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sambhogakaya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nirmanakaya|qq=}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu-ji|qq=}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8th century|qq=}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Shinto|qq=}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oharae no Kotoba|qq=}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saichō|lt=Saichō|lt=Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saicho|qq=}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kukai|qq=}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendo|qq=}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen-en|qq=}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period|qq=}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|qq=}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Shinto|qq=}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu|qq=}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abolition of Buddhism|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|Japanese book |author=Satoshi Ito|authorlink={{ill|Satoshi Ito|ja|伊藤聡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satoshi Ito|qq=}} |title=What is Shinto? |year=2012 |publisher=[[Chuo Koron Shinsha]] |series=[[Chuko Shinsho]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Ryobu Torii|ja|両部鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryobu Torii|qq=}} * {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Esoteric Buddhism}} {{Buddhism}}

On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8th century|qq=}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Shinto|qq=}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oharae no Kotoba|qq=}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saichō|lt=Saichō|lt=Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saicho|qq=}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kukai|qq=}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō|lt=Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendo|qq=}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen-en|qq=}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period|qq=}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|qq=}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Shinto|qq=}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu|qq=}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abolition of Buddhism|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

[[ja:両部神道]] [[en:Ryobu Shinto]] [[sn:Ryobu Shinto]] [[zh:兩部神道]] [[ru:Рёбу синто]] [[fr:Ryōbu shintō]] [[sn:yobu Shinto]]
<!--Ryobu Shinto--><!--Ryobu Shinto--><!--Ryobu Shinto-->

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a {{ill|Buddhist|ja|仏教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist|qq=}} interpretation of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} from the perspective of the {{ill|Shingon sect|ja|真言宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon sect|qq=}} (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}}. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhism|qq=}}, the universe is considered to be a manifestation of {{ill|Vairocana|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|13=User:Immanuelle/Vairocana|qq=}}. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}} depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the {{ill|Japanese deities|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese deities|qq=}} are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Light King|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Light King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|トヨウケビメ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|qq=}}, is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ({{ill|Brahma|ja|梵天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brahma|qq=}}), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the {{ill|Trikaya|ja|三身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Trikaya|qq=}} (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, identifying {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}} as the {{ill|Dharmakaya|ja|法身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dharmakaya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the {{ill|Sambhogakaya|ja|報身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sambhogakaya|qq=}}, and {{ill|Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the {{ill|Nirmanakaya|ja|応身|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nirmanakaya|qq=}}. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}}. Additionally, deities like {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of {{ill|Jingu-ji|ja|神宮寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu-ji|qq=}} (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late {{ill|8th century|ja|8世紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8th century|qq=}}, the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later {{ill|Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} thought.
In the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}}'s teachings were incorporated into {{ill|Sanno Shinto|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanno Shinto|qq=}}, and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted {{ill|Oharae no Kotoba|ja|大祓詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oharae no Kotoba|qq=}} and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like {{ill|Saichō|lt=Saichō|lt=Saicho|ja|最澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saicho|qq=}} and {{ill|Kukai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kukai|qq=}}, were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō|lt=Shugendo|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendo|qq=}}. These teachings were systematized in the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in {{ill|Shinsen-en|ja|神泉苑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen-en|qq=}}. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period|qq=}} onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the {{ill|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-cho period (Japan)|qq=}}, a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shinto|ja|吉田神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Shinto|qq=}}, which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the {{ill|Bakumatsu|ja|幕末|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu|qq=}} and {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Abolition of Buddhism|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abolition of Buddhism|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|qq=}} in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] interpretation of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] from the perspective of the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], the universe is considered to be a manifestation of [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]]. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]] depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the [[:en:Kami|Japanese deities]] are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, [[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Mahabrahma Light King]] ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke Omikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the [[:en:Trikaya|Trikaya]] (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], identifying [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]] as the [[:en:Dharmakāya|Dharmakaya]], {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the [[:en:Saṃbhogakāya|Sambhogakaya]], and {{ill|Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the [[:en:Nirmāṇakāya|Nirmanakaya]]. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]]. Additionally, deities like [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] and [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of [[:en:Jingū-ji|Jingu-ji]] (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late [[:en:8th century|8th century]], the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji suijaku]] (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] thought.
In the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]]'s teachings were incorporated into [[:en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|Sanno Shinto]], and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted [[:en:Ōharae no Kotoba|Oharae no Kotoba]] and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like [[:en:Saichō|Saicho]] and [[:en:Kūkai|Kukai]], were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]. These teachings were systematized in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in [[:en:Shinsenen|Shinsen-en]]. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period]] onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)]], a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]], which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] and [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], with the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] and the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

[[ja:両部神道]] [[en:Ryobu Shinto]] [[sn:Ryobu Shinto]] [[zh:兩部神道]] [[ru:Рёбу синто]] [[fr:Ryōbu shintō]] [[sn:yobu Shinto]]

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] interpretation of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] from the perspective of the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], the universe is considered to be a manifestation of [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]]. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]] depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the [[:en:Kami|Japanese deities]] are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, [[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Mahabrahma Light King]] ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke Omikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the [[:en:Trikaya|Trikaya]] (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], identifying [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]] as the [[:en:Dharmakāya|Dharmakaya]], {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the [[:en:Saṃbhogakāya|Sambhogakaya]], and {{ill|Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the [[:en:Nirmāṇakāya|Nirmanakaya]]. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]]. Additionally, deities like [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] and [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of [[:en:Jingū-ji|Jingu-ji]] (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late [[:en:8th century|8th century]], the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji suijaku]] (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] thought.
In the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]]'s teachings were incorporated into [[:en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|Sanno Shinto]], and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted [[:en:Ōharae no Kotoba|Oharae no Kotoba]] and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like [[:en:Saichō|Saicho]] and [[:en:Kūkai|Kukai]], were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]. These teachings were systematized in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in [[:en:Shinsenen|Shinsen-en]]. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period]] onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)]], a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]], which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] and [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], with the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] and the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] interpretation of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] from the perspective of the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], the universe is considered to be a manifestation of [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]]. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]] depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the [[:en:Kami|Japanese deities]] are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, [[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Mahabrahma Light King]] ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke Omikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the [[:en:Trikaya|Trikaya]] (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], identifying [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]] as the [[:en:Dharmakāya|Dharmakaya]], {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the [[:en:Saṃbhogakāya|Sambhogakaya]], and {{ill|Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the [[:en:Nirmāṇakāya|Nirmanakaya]]. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]]. Additionally, deities like [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] and [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of [[:en:Jingū-ji|Jingu-ji]] (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late [[:en:8th century|8th century]], the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji suijaku]] (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] thought.
In the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]]'s teachings were incorporated into [[:en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|Sanno Shinto]], and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted [[:en:Ōharae no Kotoba|Oharae no Kotoba]] and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like [[:en:Saichō|Saicho]] and [[:en:Kūkai|Kukai]], were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]. These teachings were systematized in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in [[:en:Shinsenen|Shinsen-en]]. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period]] onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)]], a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]], which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] and [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], with the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] and the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|Japanese book |author=Satoshi Ito|authorlink={{ill|Satoshi Ito|ja|伊藤聡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Satoshi Ito|qq=}} |title=What is Shinto? |year=2012 |publisher=[[Chuo Koron Shinsha]] |series=[[Chuko Shinsho]] |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8 }} == See Also == * {{ill|Ryobu Torii|ja|両部鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryobu Torii|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Esoteric Buddhism}} {{Buddhism}}

These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like [[:en:Saichō|Saicho]] and [[:en:Kūkai|Kukai]], were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]. These teachings were systematized in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in [[:en:Shinsenen|Shinsen-en]]. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period]] onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)]], a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]], which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] and [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], with the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] and the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.

[[ja:両部神道]] [[en:Ryobu Shinto]] [[sn:Ryobu Shinto]] [[zh:兩部神道]] [[ru:Рёбу синто]] [[fr:Ryōbu shintō]] [[sn:yobu Shinto]]
<!--Ryobu Shinto--><!--Ryobu Shinto--><!--Ryobu Shinto--> [[Category:Vajrayana]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Heian period]] [[Category:Japanese Vajrayana Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto schools of thought]]

{{Multiple issues|source=August 28, 2017|ref=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Kongokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Diamond Realm]] [[File:Taizokai.jpg|thumb|220px|Mandala of the Womb Realm]] '''Ryobu Shinto''' (両部神道) is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] interpretation of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] from the perspective of the [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] (Esoteric Buddhism), based on the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]. It is also known as '''Ryobu Syncretic Shinto''' (りょうぶしゅうごうしんとう).
== Overview == In [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]], the universe is considered to be a manifestation of [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]]. This is expressed in the rituals of the {{ill|Diamond Realm Mandala|ja|金剛界曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Diamond Realm Mandala|qq=}} and the {{ill|Womb Realm Mandala|ja|胎蔵曼陀羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Womb Realm Mandala|qq=}}, centered on Vairocana. The Buddhas and [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]] depicted in these two mandalas are regarded as the original forms (honji), and the [[:en:Kami|Japanese deities]] are interpreted as their manifestations (suijaku).
=== Philosophy === In Ryobu Shinto, the deity of the Inner Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Womb Realm, [[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Mahabrahma Light King]] ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Nichiten|ja|日天子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiten|qq=}}. On the other hand, the deity of the Outer Shrine, [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke Omikami]], is identified with the Vairocana of the Diamond Realm, {{ill|Mahabrahma Abandonment King|ja|尸棄大梵天王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahabrahma Abandonment King|qq=}} ([[:en:Brahmā (Buddhism)|Brahma]]), and {{ill|Chandra|ja|月天子|lt=Gatsuten|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chandra|qq=}}. It is believed that the Inner and Outer Shrines of Ise form a unity that manifests Vairocana, known as the "One Light of the Two Shrines" theory. Ryobu Shinto aims to explain the ultimate unity of gods and Buddhas through this idea<ref name="itou">{{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Satoshi Ito|title=What is Shinto?|publisher=Chuo Koron Shinsha|series=Chuko Shinsho|year=2012}}</ref>.
Furthermore, it applies the [[:en:Trikaya|Trikaya]] (three bodies) of the Buddhas in Buddhism to the three gods in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], identifying [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]] as the [[:en:Dharmakāya|Dharmakaya]], {{ill|Kunisatsuchi|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as the [[:en:Saṃbhogakāya|Sambhogakaya]], and {{ill|Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono-no-Mikoto|lt=Toyokumono|ja|豊斟渟尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokumono|qq=}} as the [[:en:Nirmāṇakāya|Nirmanakaya]]. It also asserts that the unity of these three gods corresponds to the Esoteric Buddhist deity, Vairocana.
The seven generations of gods in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] are equated with the {{ill|Seven Buddhas of the past|ja|七仏通誡偈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven Buddhas of the past|qq=}}, and they are also associated with the stars of the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]]. Additionally, deities like [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] and [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], as well as the upper and lower shrines of {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}}, are likened to the two mandalas.
== History == The origins of Ryobu Shinto can be traced back to the introduction of Buddhism.
With the {{ill|Introduction of Buddhism|ja|仏教公伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Introduction of Buddhism|qq=}}, Shinto, the native faith of Japan, was greatly influenced. The deities of Japan were seen as desiring liberation through Buddhist teachings, leading to the recitation of Buddhist scriptures before them, the construction of [[:en:Jingū-ji|Jingu-ji]] (shrine temples) within shrine precincts, and the creation of {{ill|Shinzo|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzo|qq=}} (statues of deities) influenced by Buddhist icons.
By the late [[:en:8th century|8th century]], the belief arose that Japanese deities were identical to Buddhas, leading to the concept of [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji suijaku]] (the original form of the Buddhas appearing as Japanese deities to save people), which became the foundation of later [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] thought.
In the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Buddhist theories explaining Shinto were developed by monks. Of the two major Esoteric Buddhist sects at the time, [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]]'s teachings were incorporated into [[:en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|Sanno Shinto]], and Shingon teachings were incorporated into '''Ryobu Shinto'''.
Both schools interpreted [[:en:Ōharae no Kotoba|Oharae no Kotoba]] and the deities and shrines mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|ja|記紀神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki and Nihon Shoki|qq=}} using the doctrines of Esoteric Buddhism, which was mainstream in the Buddhist world at that time.
These Shinto writings, attributed to figures like [[:en:Saichō|Saicho]] and [[:en:Kūkai|Kukai]], were passed down as secret teachings within shrines and were also known to some extent through [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]. These teachings were systematized in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and gave rise to many Shinto theories in later generations.
Among these Shinto writings, the most influential in later generations was the "Reikiki," said to be a secret tradition received by Emperor Daigo from a dragon maiden who appeared in [[:en:Shinsenen|Shinsen-en]]. This text compiles deep esoteric teachings related to Ise Grand Shrine and came to be regarded as one of the most important scriptures of medieval Shinto, along with the "Nihon Shoki," from the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period]] onwards<ref name="itou"/>.
=== Influence === Ryobu Shinto had a significant impact on the development of later Shinto theories, becoming the mainstream of syncretic Shinto in the medieval period, leading to the emergence of many sub-schools such as {{ill|Goryu Shinto|ja|御流神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryu Shinto|qq=}} and {{ill|Miwa Shinto|ja|三輪神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shinto|qq=}}.
However, from the late Kamakura period to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-cho period (Japan)]], a reaction against Buddhist interpretations of Shinto arose, leading to the emergence of schools like {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shinto]], which advocated the idea that the gods were the original forms and Buddhas were their temporary manifestations ({{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku theory|ja|神本仏迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku theory|qq=}}). By the Edo period, these became the mainstream doctrines of Shinto.
During the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] and [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], with the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] and the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] in the first year of Meiji (1868), Ryobu Shinto suffered a devastating blow and lost its position as the mainstream of Shinto doctrine.
